entries.definition: If separate amounts add up to a total amount, together they form that total.
examples: The bills add up to over £500 this month.
All the small expenses add up to a lot of money.
The scores add up to 100 points.

entries.definition: To combine to produce a particular result or effect; to amount to a total.
examples: Small savings add up to significant amounts over time.
These expenses add up to more than our monthly budget.
All the evidence adds up to a clear conclusion.

entries.definition: To reply rudely to someone who has more authority than you.
examples: The student was punished for answering back to the teacher.
Don't answer back when your manager gives you feedback.
She regretted answering back to her parents in anger.

entries.definition: To invite someone to go with you to a cinema, restaurant, etc. because you want to start a romantic or sexual relationship with them.
examples: He finally asked her out after weeks of hesitation.
Would you ever ask out someone you met online?
She was thrilled when he asked her out to dinner.

entries.definition: To stop asking for something or saying that you will do something because a lot of people oppose you.
examples: The government refused to back down on the controversial policy.
She never backs down from a challenge.
After weeks of protests, the company finally backed down.

entries.definition: To make a copy of information on your computer.
examples: Make sure to back up your files regularly.
I back up all my photos to the cloud.
Have you backed up your work before updating the software?

entries.definition: To give support to someone by telling other people that you agree with them.
examples: She backed up her colleague's claims during the meeting.
The evidence backs up his theory completely.
Will you back me up if I propose this idea?

entries.definition: To prove that something is true or correct.
examples: Subsequent research has borne out our initial hypothesis about climate change impacts.
The financial results bore out the analysts' predictions of a market downturn.
Her concerns were eventually borne out when the project failed to meet its objectives.
The independent audit bore out allegations of financial misconduct within the corporation.
Long-term studies have borne out the effectiveness of early intervention programmes.
Her intuition was borne out when the predicted market correction finally occurred.

entries.definition: To suddenly become unconscious.
examples: She blacked out from exhaustion after the marathon.
The heat caused several spectators to black out.
He blacked out briefly after the accident.

entries.definition: If someone or something blends in, they are similar to the other people or objects around them.
examples: The new building was designed to blend in with the historic surroundings.
She tried to blend in with the crowd at the party.
His accent helped him blend in with the locals.

entries.definition: To say something suddenly and without thinking about the effect it will have, usually because you are nervous or excited.
examples: She blurted out the secret before realising everyone was listening.
In his excitement, he blurted out the surprise party plans.
Try not to blurt out your opinions during the formal meeting.

entries.definition: To cover a window or door with wooden boards.
examples: The abandoned house had all its windows boarded up.
Shop owners boarded up their windows before the storm.
The building was boarded up after the fire.

entries.definition: To divide something such as a total amount into separate parts.
examples: Let me break down the costs for you item by item.
The report breaks down the data by region.
Can you break down the project into smaller tasks?

entries.definition: If two people break up, they end their romantic relationship.
examples: They broke up after dating for three years.
It's hard to break up with someone you still care about.
My sister and her boyfriend broke up last month.

entries.definition: To become sunnier; to start looking or feeling happier; to add more colour or light to something.
examples: The weather brightened up in the afternoon.
Her face brightened up when she heard the good news.
Some flowers would really brighten up this room.

entries.definition: To make something happen, especially to cause changes in a situation.
examples: The new policy brought about significant improvements.
What brought about this sudden change in attitude?
Technology has brought about revolutionary changes in education.

entries.definition: To make someone or something show a quality that they have; to produce a new product.
examples: The tech giant is bringing out a new smartphone next month.
Competition brings out the best in some athletes.
The company brought out an innovative line of sustainable products.

entries.definition: To create a situation in which people meet and do something together.
examples: The conference brought together experts from around the world.
Music has the power to bring together people from all backgrounds.
The project brought together volunteers from different organisations.

entries.definition: To practise and improve your skills or knowledge of something.
examples: I need to brush up on my Spanish before the trip.
She's brushing up on her maths for the exam.
You should brush up on your interview skills.

entries.definition: To gradually develop or increase something over time.
examples: She built up the business from nothing over ten years.
We need to build up our customer base before expanding.
He's been building up his investment portfolio steadily.

entries.definition: To talk about someone or something in a very positive way so that people are impressed with them.
examples: The marketing team has been building up the new product launch for months to generate maximum interest.
Critics accused the media of building up the company's achievements while ignoring its shortcomings.
Before the merger, both parties worked to build up confidence among stakeholders and investors.
The media has been building up the candidate as a transformative leader for the nation.
The company's PR team worked tirelessly to build up the brand's reputation internationally.
Don't build up expectations too high—we need to manage stakeholder perceptions carefully.

entries.definition: To accidentally hit against something; to meet someone unexpectedly.
examples: I bumped into an old friend at the supermarket.
Be careful not to bump into the glass door.
She bumped into her ex-boyfriend at the party.

entries.definition: To give someone money so that they do not act against you.
examples: There were allegations that the corporation attempted to buy off local officials to avoid regulatory scrutiny.
The whistleblower refused to be bought off despite numerous financial incentives offered by the company.
Some believe the settlement was merely an attempt to buy off the plaintiffs before damaging evidence emerged.

entries.definition: To pay money to your business partner so that you can control all of a business you previously owned together.
examples: After years of disagreements, she decided to buy out her co-founder and run the company independently.
The private equity firm bought out the remaining shareholders to take the company private.
He was forced to buy out his partner when their business relationship became untenable.

entries.definition: To buy large amounts of something or all of it that is available.
examples: Investors are buying up properties in the city centre.
The corporation bought up all its competitors.
Speculators bought up the entire stock of rare coins.

entries.definition: To stop something from having any effect; to neutralise.
examples: The gains were cancelled out by unexpected losses.
His efforts to save money were cancelled out by rising prices.
The positive and negative factors tend to cancel out.

entries.definition: To take something that you earn or are given in one year into the next one.
examples: Unused holiday days can be carried over to next year.
The profits were carried over into the following quarter.
Some tax benefits can be carried over for several years.

entries.definition: To become popular or fashionable; to understand something after initially not understanding it.
examples: The new trend caught on quickly among young people.
It took me a while, but I finally caught on to what she meant.
Streaming services caught on faster than anyone predicted.

entries.definition: If something centres around someone or something, they are its main subject of attention.
examples: The discussion centred around budget concerns.
Her research centres around climate change.
The novel centres around a family in 19th-century England.

entries.definition: To find or see someone or something when you did not expect to.
examples: While researching the archives, I chanced upon a letter that completely changed our understanding of the case.
She chanced upon her former professor at an international conference in Vienna.
The discovery was made when scientists chanced upon an unusual pattern in the data.

entries.definition: To move things so that they are in different places or positions.
examples: She changed around the furniture to create more space.
Let's change around the seating arrangement for the meeting.
They changed around the office layout to improve workflow.

entries.definition: To stop being in one state and start being in another; to put on different clothes.
examples: I need to change into something more comfortable.
She changed into her pyjamas after dinner.
The frog changed into a prince in the fairy tale.

entries.definition: To take off the clothes you are wearing and put on different ones.
examples: He changed out of his work clothes as soon as he got home.
Can I change out of this dress? It's too tight.
She changed out of her uniform after school.

entries.definition: To examine someone or something in order to be certain that everything is correct, true, or satisfactory.
examples: Can you check out this report before I send it?
I'll check out the new restaurant everyone's talking about.
The doctor will check out your symptoms.

entries.definition: If information checks out, you feel that it is true after examining it.
examples: Her alibi checked out when police reviewed the footage.
We need to make sure these figures check out before publishing.
The references on his CV all checked out.

entries.definition: To reach a particular number or amount; to accumulate.
examples: The company has clocked up record profits this year.
She's clocked up over a million air miles.
They've clocked up considerable debts over the years.

entries.definition: To lock the doors of a building or business; to cease operations for the day.
examples: We close up at nine o'clock on weekdays.
The shop owner was closing up when the last customer arrived.
Make sure to close up properly before leaving.

entries.definition: If people club together, each of them gives some money so all the money collected can be used to buy something.
examples: We clubbed together to buy her a retirement gift.
The neighbours clubbed together to fund the street party.
Let's club together and get him something really special.

entries.definition: To change your opinion or decision because someone has persuaded you.
examples: He eventually came around to my way of thinking.
Give her time—she'll come round to the idea.
The board finally came around to accepting the proposal.

entries.definition: To meet someone or find something by chance.
examples: I came across an old photo while cleaning.
She came across as very friendly during the interview.
Have you come across any useful websites?

entries.definition: To cause a disagreement or argument between people.
examples: Don't let money come between you and your friends.
Nothing should come between family members.
The inheritance dispute came between the siblings.

entries.definition: To become available to buy or see; to have a particular result or end in a particular way.
examples: Her new book comes out next month.
The photos came out really well.
When does the new phone model come out?

entries.definition: To be spoken, heard, or understood in a particular way.
examples: I didn't mean to be rude—it just came out wrong.
Her frustration came out clearly in her tone of voice.
The apology came out sounding insincere despite his best efforts.

entries.definition: To say something suddenly, usually something that surprises or shocks people.
examples: She came out with a brilliant solution no one had considered.
He always comes out with the most unexpected observations.
During the interview, the candidate came out with some controversial views.

entries.definition: To go to a place where someone is, especially their house, in order to visit them.
examples: Why don't you come round for coffee tomorrow?
My neighbours often come round in the evening.
Feel free to come round whenever you like.

entries.definition: To become conscious again after being unconscious.
examples: She came round a few minutes after fainting.
The patient came round slowly after the surgery.
When he finally came round, he couldn't remember anything.

entries.definition: To think of something such as an idea or a plan.
examples: She came up with a brilliant solution to the problem.
Can you come up with any ideas for the party?
We need to come up with a new strategy.

entries.definition: To become cooler or make something cooler.
examples: Let the soup cool down before you eat it.
We went swimming to cool down after the long walk.
The weather is finally starting to cool down.

entries.definition: To stop people from entering an area by putting something such as a rope around it.
examples: Police cordoned off the crime scene immediately.
The street was cordoned off for the filming.
They cordoned off the damaged section of the building.

entries.definition: To begin to realise or understand something, especially after being slow to do so.
examples: She eventually cottoned on to the fact that they were joking.
It took him ages to cotton on to the subtle hints.
The audience finally cottoned on to the comedian's dry humour.

entries.definition: To start dealing with someone or something much more strictly.
examples: Police are cracking down on drink-driving.
The school is cracking down on bullying.
Authorities have started to crack down on tax evasion.

entries.definition: To laugh a lot or make someone laugh a lot.
examples: His deadpan delivery of the anecdote had the entire audience creasing up.
The children creased up when their grandfather started his impersonations.
Her witty observations about office life never fail to crease me up.

entries.definition: To move towards someone quietly and slowly, especially because you want to surprise them.
examples: The cat crept up on the mouse silently.
Don't creep up on me like that—you scared me!
Old age has a way of creeping up on you.

entries.definition: To appear or happen suddenly or unexpectedly.
examples: Unexpected problems cropped up during the renovation.
Opportunities tend to crop up when you least expect them.
New evidence has cropped up that changes everything.

entries.definition: To move to a particular place at the same time as a lot of other people.
examples: Fans crowded around the singer after the concert.
People crowded around the window to see what was happening.
Children crowded around the ice cream truck.

entries.definition: To reduce the amount of something, especially money that you spend.
examples: We've had to cut back on luxury items this year.
The company is cutting back on staff training budgets.
They're cutting back their spending after the recession.

entries.definition: To remove something from a larger piece by cutting.
examples: She cut out pictures from the magazine for her project.
I need to cut out a hole in the wall for the pipe.
He cut out a piece of cardboard for the model.

entries.definition: To stop eating something or doing something, especially because it is bad for your health.
examples: The doctor advised me to cut out sugar completely.
She's trying to cut out caffeine from her diet.
You should cut out smoking if you want to feel healthier.

entries.definition: To become weaker or less common and then disappear completely.
examples: Many traditional crafts are in danger of dying out.
The dinosaurs died out millions of years ago.
Local dialects are slowly dying out in many regions.

entries.definition: To dig holes in an area of land; to find information by searching; to remove something from underground.
examples: Archaeologists dug up ancient artefacts at the site.
The journalist dug up some surprising facts about the company.
We dug up the old pipes to replace them.

entries.definition: To start doing something in a very enthusiastic way, often without much preparation.
examples: She dived in and started reorganising the entire department.
Don't be afraid to dive in and try something new.
He tends to dive in without considering the consequences.

entries.definition: To get rid of something; to abolish.
examples: The company did away with traditional hierarchies.
Many countries have done away with the death penalty.
Let's do away with unnecessary paperwork.

entries.definition: To fasten an item of clothing.
examples: Can you do up the buttons on my dress?
He did up his coat because it was cold.
She did up her shoelaces before running.

entries.definition: To repair, paint, and improve an old building, car, boat, etc.
examples: They're doing up an old farmhouse in the countryside.
He did up a vintage car and sold it for a profit.
The restaurant was recently done up with a modern interior.

entries.definition: To use information or your experience of something to help you do something.
examples: The author draws on extensive field research conducted over two decades.
In developing our strategy, we drew on the expertise of specialists from multiple disciplines.
She drew on her experience as a diplomat to navigate the complex negotiations.
Successful leaders draw on diverse perspectives to make well-informed decisions.
The architect drew on traditional techniques while incorporating innovative materials.
In times of crisis, organisations must draw on their institutional knowledge and resilience.

entries.definition: To try to make people support you or buy something from you.
examples: The sales team was tasked with drumming up interest among potential investors before the IPO.
Despite extensive efforts to drum up support, the proposal failed to gain traction with the board.
She spent months drumming up business for her consulting firm in a highly competitive market.

entries.definition: If something dries up or is dried up, all the water comes out of it.
examples: The river dried up during the summer drought.
Leave the dishes to dry up on the rack.
The puddles dried up quickly in the hot sun.

entries.definition: To stop being available; to become completely dry.
examples: Investment in the sector has dried up completely.
The river dried up during the severe drought.
Job opportunities have dried up in this industry.

entries.definition: To be in a particular place or state after doing something or because of doing it.
examples: We got lost and ended up in a tiny village.
If you keep eating like that, you'll end up sick.
She ended up becoming a famous artist.

entries.definition: To disappear slowly; to gradually become less distinct.
examples: The music faded away as we walked further from the party.
Her enthusiasm faded away once she realised the difficulty.
Old memories tend to fade away over time.

entries.definition: To move more slowly than other people so that you are behind them.
examples: She fell behind the rest of the group during the hike.
Don't fall behind—we need to stay together!
He fell behind in the race and couldn't catch up.

entries.definition: To make less progress or be less successful than other people who are doing a similar job or activity.
examples: If you miss too many classes, you'll fall behind.
The company fell behind its competitors in innovation.
Students who fall behind often struggle to catch up.

entries.definition: To be able to understand something or solve a problem; to understand what someone is like and why they behave in a certain way.
examples: I can't figure out how this machine works.
It took me hours to figure out the puzzle.
She finally figured out why he was upset.

entries.definition: To clean, repair, or decorate something.
examples: We need to fix up the spare room before guests arrive.
She fixed up the garden beautifully for the party.
The community fixed up the playground for the children.

entries.definition: To add more details or information to a plan or idea.
examples: The committee will meet next week to flesh out the proposal before presenting it to the board.
Your concept is promising, but you'll need to flesh out the implementation details.
The researchers plan to flesh out their preliminary findings with additional case studies.
The team will need to flesh out the proposal with concrete implementation timelines.
Your initial concept is compelling—now let's flesh out the supporting arguments.
The committee requested that we flesh out the risk assessment section more thoroughly.

entries.definition: To try to find out more about something or do something more to deal with it.
examples: We need to follow up on these leads immediately.
The doctor asked me to follow up with a specialist.
I'll follow up with an email after the meeting.

entries.definition: If news gets around, a lot of people hear it.
examples: Word got around about the company's financial troubles.
News of the scandal got around very quickly.
It didn't take long for the rumour to get around.

entries.definition: To make people understand something; to communicate an idea effectively.
examples: The teacher used diagrams to get across the complex concept.
It's difficult to get across how important this deadline is.
His presentation failed to get across the urgency of the situation.

entries.definition: To make someone feel sad or lose hope.
examples: The constant rain is really getting me down.
Don't let negative comments get you down.
The lack of progress was getting the whole team down.

entries.definition: To be elected for a political job or position.
examples: She got in as a councillor in the local elections.
If the opposition party gets in, policies will change dramatically.
He's hoping to get in as the new union representative.

entries.definition: If a train, plane, etc. gets into a place, it arrives there.
examples: What time does the train get into London?
The flight gets into Paris at midnight.
We got into the station just as it started raining.

entries.definition: To have a particular period of time as a holiday or break from work.
examples: I'm getting off early today for a doctor's appointment.
She gets off two weeks for Christmas every year.
We rarely get off before six o'clock.

entries.definition: To find a way to solve or deal with a difficult problem; to start to feel happy after something bad.
examples: It took her months to get over the disappointment.
We need to get over this obstacle before proceeding.
He finally got over his fear of public speaking.

entries.definition: To finish dealing with some work; to manage to deal with a difficult situation.
examples: I've got a lot of paperwork to get through today.
How did you get through such a difficult period?
She managed to get through the interview without any problems.

entries.definition: To be connected to a place by telephone.
examples: I've been trying to get through to the office all morning.
The lines were busy, but I finally got through.
Can you get through to customer service?

entries.definition: To make someone understand what you are trying to say; to reach someone by phone.
examples: I couldn't get through to him—he just wouldn't listen.
It's impossible to get through to customer service today.
How can I get through to you that this is important?

entries.definition: To stop competing or arguing and accept that you cannot win.
examples: After hours of negotiation, they finally gave in to our demands.
Don't give in—keep fighting for what you believe in.
She refused to give in to pressure from her colleagues.

entries.definition: To become lost or go to the wrong place; to behave wrongly.
examples: Several packages went astray during delivery.
The investigation went astray when false leads emerged.
Good intentions can sometimes go astray.

entries.definition: To produce a particular reaction; to be received in a certain way.
examples: The announcement went down well with investors.
His joke went down badly with the serious audience.
How did your proposal go down with the committee?

entries.definition: To choose something as a subject of study or as your career; to enjoy a particular thing.
examples: She's thinking of going in for medicine.
He doesn't go in for team sports much.
I've never gone in for extreme diets.

entries.definition: To leave a place, especially for a particular purpose.
examples: She went off to work early this morning.
He went off on holiday without telling anyone.
They went off to explore the old town.

entries.definition: To stop liking someone or something.
examples: I've gone off coffee lately—it tastes too bitter.
She went off him after discovering his true character.
Many people go off their favourite foods during pregnancy.

entries.definition: If two things go together, they seem good, natural, or attractive in combination.
examples: Those shoes and that bag go together perfectly.
Do you think this tie and shirt go together?
Blue and white always go together well.

entries.definition: If something or someone grows on you, you start to like them more.
examples: The neighbourhood has really grown on me over the years.
Classical music didn't appeal to me at first, but it's grown on me.
He was annoying initially, but he's grown on me.

entries.definition: To spend time in a particular place or with particular people.
examples: We used to hang out at the mall after school.
I love hanging out with my friends on weekends.
Where do you usually hang out on Friday nights?

entries.definition: To prevent someone from going somewhere; to prevent something from taking place.
examples: Security guards headed off the protestors before they reached the building.
We need to head off any potential problems early.
She headed off the argument by changing the subject.

entries.definition: To make something hot; to become hot.
examples: Can you heat up the leftovers for dinner?
The room started to heat up when we turned on the heater.
Heat up the oil before adding the vegetables.

entries.definition: To criticise someone who has criticised you; to deliberately hurt someone who has hurt you.
examples: The politician hit back at critics during the press conference.
The company hit back with a statement defending their practices.
She hit back at the accusations in a lengthy social media post.

entries.definition: To discover something by chance; to suddenly have an idea.
examples: She hit upon the solution while walking in the park.
Scientists hit upon a breakthrough after years of research.
He hit upon the idea of using renewable energy sources.

entries.definition: To stop someone or something from moving forwards; to prevent progress.
examples: The police held back the crowd during the protest.
Don't let fear hold you back from pursuing your dreams.
Barriers held back the flood waters for several hours.

entries.definition: To solve problems or difficulties, especially small ones.
examples: We need to iron out a few logistical issues before we can proceed with the launch.
The negotiating teams worked through the night to iron out the remaining disagreements.
Once we've ironed out the technical glitches, the system should run smoothly.

entries.definition: To continue to do something; to move at the same speed as someone or something.
examples: Keep up the good work!
I can't keep up with all the new technology.
She walks so fast that I can barely keep up.

entries.definition: To put information into a computer or other electronic machine using keys or a keyboard.
examples: Key in your password to access the system.
She keyed in the customer's details carefully.
Please key in the code shown on the screen.

entries.definition: To begin with something; to start an event or activity.
examples: The conference kicks off with a keynote speech tomorrow.
We'll kick off the meeting with a review of last month's figures.
The sale kicks off at midnight.

entries.definition: To destroy living things so that most or all of them are dead.
examples: Pollution is killing off marine life in the bay.
The frost killed off most of the crops.
Antibiotics can kill off beneficial bacteria.

entries.definition: To stop working, usually at the end of a working day.
examples: We usually knock off around five o'clock.
Let's knock off early today and go for a drink.
The factory workers knock off at the sound of the bell.

entries.definition: To destroy a building or wall.
examples: They knocked down the old warehouse to build flats.
We're planning to tear down the garden shed.
The council pulled down the abandoned factory.

entries.definition: To start working hard, especially when you should have done this earlier.
examples: It's time to knuckle down and finish the project.
She finally knuckled down after months of procrastination.
The team needs to knuckle down if we're going to meet the deadline.

entries.definition: To speak angrily to or against someone; to try to hit or attack someone suddenly and violently.
examples: He lashed out at reporters for invading his privacy.
She tends to lash out when she feels threatened.
The cornered animal lashed out at anyone who came close.

entries.definition: To end someone's employment, especially temporarily, because there is not enough work for them.
examples: The factory laid off hundreds of workers during the recession.
She was laid off when the company downsized.
Many employees fear being laid off due to automation.

entries.definition: To relax and enjoy yourself, doing no work.
examples: I spent the whole Sunday lazing around the house.
We lazed around by the pool all afternoon.
Sometimes it's nice to just laze around and do nothing.

entries.definition: To talk about something that is intended to be a secret; to reveal information.
examples: Don't let on that we know about their engagement.
She never let on how much the criticism had hurt her.
He didn't let on that he had already seen the confidential report.

entries.definition: If something lies ahead, it is going to happen to you in the future.
examples: Exciting opportunities lie ahead for graduates.
No one knows what challenges lie ahead.
A bright future lies ahead for the young researcher.

entries.definition: To give something a more interesting quality; to make something more exciting.
examples: Some music would liven up this party.
He told jokes to liven up the presentation.
The new artwork really livened up the office space.

entries.definition: To lock all the doors and windows of a building so that no one can get in.
examples: Make sure you lock up before you leave the house.
He locked up the shop at 6 p.m. every day.
Did you remember to lock up last night?

entries.definition: To put someone in a prison; to confine or imprison.
examples: The criminals were locked up for twenty years.
They should lock up people who commit such serious crimes.
He spent five years locked up before being released.

entries.definition: To change someone or something so that they become something else.
examples: The warehouse was made into luxury apartments.
She made the old dress into a fashionable new outfit.
The novel was made into a successful film.

entries.definition: To see, hear, or understand someone or something with difficulty; to suggest or imply something.
examples: I could barely make out what he was saying over the noise.
She made out that the project was her idea when it wasn't.
Can you make out the figures in this faded photograph?

entries.definition: To change or improve the appearance of someone or something.
examples: The stylists made over the participants on the TV show.
They made over the company's image with new branding.
The garden was completely made over in just one weekend.

entries.definition: To work at different times from usual because you have not worked enough at the normal times.
examples: I need to make up the hours I missed yesterday.
Can I make up the class next week?
She's making up for lost time by working weekends.

entries.definition: To come together with someone, either unexpectedly or as planned.
examples: Let's meet up for lunch next week.
We met up at the train station before the concert.
I'm planning to meet up with some old classmates.

entries.definition: To behave in a silly way; to spend time doing things in a relaxed way.
examples: Stop messing around and focus on your homework!
The kids were messing about in the garden.
We spent the afternoon messing around with old photos.

entries.definition: To make a mistake or do something badly; to make something dirty or untidy.
examples: I really messed up the job interview.
Don't mess up my room—I just cleaned it!
He messed up the recipe by adding too much salt.

entries.definition: To be the cause of someone's physical, emotional, or mental problems; to make mistakes or ruin something.
examples: The divorce really messed her up for years.
I completely messed up the presentation.
Don't mess up this opportunity—it's too important.

entries.definition: To put things together without any order; to think that one person or thing is another.
examples: I always mix up their names because they're twins.
She mixed up the files on her desk.
Don't mix up the ingredients before measuring them.

entries.definition: To get much larger; to accumulate to a significant amount.
examples: Credit card bills can mount up quickly if you're not careful.
The repair costs started to mount up alarmingly.
Evidence against the suspect continued to mount up.

entries.definition: To start living in a different house or flat (with someone).
examples: They're moving in together next month.
We moved in last week and we're still unpacking.
She moved in with her boyfriend after six months.

entries.definition: To leave one place and travel to another.
examples: After a week in Paris, we moved on to Rome.
The tour group moved on to the next attraction.
We can't stay here forever—it's time to move on.

entries.definition: To stop discussing or doing something and begin discussing or doing something different.
examples: Let's move on to the next agenda item.
She decided to move on from her past mistakes.
It's time to move on and focus on the future.

entries.definition: To permanently leave the house or flat where you live or the place where you have your business.
examples: He moved out of his parents' house when he was eighteen.
The previous tenants moved out last month.
I need to move out by the end of the week.

entries.definition: To change your position in order to make space for someone or something.
examples: Can you move over so I can sit down?
He moved over to let her through.
Move over a bit—I can't see the screen.

entries.definition: To think carefully about something over a period of time.
examples: I need some time to mull over your proposal before deciding.
She spent the weekend mulling over her career options.
He mulled over the implications of the contract for several days.

entries.definition: To open a locked door, container, or building.
examples: The shop opens up at 9 a.m. every morning.
Can you open up the garage for me?
They opened up the old house after years of it being closed.

entries.definition: To make it easier to travel or do business in a country; to talk more about personal feelings.
examples: The trade agreement opened up new markets in Asia.
She finally opened up about her concerns at work.
The internet has opened up countless business opportunities.

entries.definition: To decide not to take part in something, or to stop taking part in it.
examples: Several countries opted out of the agreement.
You can opt out of receiving marketing emails at any time.
He decided to opt out of the company pension scheme.

entries.definition: To hide a problem or disagreement rather than finding a satisfactory solution to it.
examples: The government's new policy appears to simply paper over the underlying structural inequalities.
Rather than addressing the fundamental issues, management chose to paper over the conflict between departments.
Critics argued that the peace agreement merely papered over deep-seated tensions that would inevitably resurface.
The reconciliation process merely papered over deep-rooted grievances within the organization.
You can't simply paper over fundamental design flaws and expect the system to function.
The diplomatic summit papered over tensions that would later escalate into open conflict.

entries.definition: To die (used to avoid saying 'die' when you think this might upset someone).
examples: His grandmother passed away peacefully in her sleep.
The famous author passed on after a long illness.
We were devastated when our beloved pet passed away.

entries.definition: To give someone something, for example a message, that someone else has given you.
examples: Could you pass on a message to your manager?
She passed on the information to the team.
Please pass on my thanks to everyone involved.

entries.definition: To become friends with someone again after a disagreement; to give basic medical treatment.
examples: They managed to patch up their differences after the argument.
The nurse patched up his wound before the doctor arrived.
It took years, but they finally patched up their friendship.

entries.definition: To give someone the same amount of money that you borrowed from them.
examples: I'll pay you back next week when I get paid.
She paid back the loan in six months.
Don't forget to pay back the money you owe me.

entries.definition: To spend or pay money, especially a lot of money.
examples: We've paid out a fortune on home improvements.
The insurance company refused to pay out on the claim.
They're paying out millions in compensation.

entries.definition: To gradually stop using something; to discontinue over a period of time.
examples: The company is phasing out its older product lines.
Many countries are phasing out single-use plastics.
They plan to phase out coal power by 2030.

entries.definition: To keep treating someone badly or unfairly, especially by criticising them.
examples: Why do you always pick on your younger brother?
She felt that her manager was picking on her unfairly.
Stop picking on him—he's doing his best.

entries.definition: To improve; to learn a new skill or start a habit without intending to.
examples: Business is starting to pick up after the slow season.
She picked up French while living in Paris.
I picked up some bad habits at my last job.

entries.definition: To learn the truth about something by considering all the separate bits of information you have.
examples: Detectives pieced together the events leading up to the crime.
She pieced together what had happened from eyewitness accounts.
It took months to piece together the company's financial history.

entries.definition: If something piles up, the amount of it increases a lot; to accumulate.
examples: Work has been piling up while I was on holiday.
The evidence against him continues to pile up.
Bills can pile up quickly if you ignore them.

entries.definition: To make someone give clear details or a decision about something.
examples: It's been difficult to pin down the exact cause of the system failure.
I've been trying to pin him down on a delivery date, but he keeps being evasive.
The investigators worked tirelessly to pin down the facts of the case.

entries.definition: To cause difficulties or pain for someone; if children play up, they behave badly.
examples: My back has been playing up again recently.
The car's engine has been playing up for weeks.
The children always play up when we have guests.

entries.definition: To continue doing something in a determined way despite difficulties, opposition, or interruptions.
examples: Despite the setbacks, the team pressed on with the project.
The government decided to press ahead with the controversial reforms.
She pressed ahead despite criticism from her colleagues.

entries.definition: To stop something from falling by putting something under it or against it.
examples: She propped up the bookshelf with wooden blocks.
The government propped up failing banks during the crisis.
He propped up his injured leg on a pillow.

entries.definition: To help a government, system, or organisation continue to exist, especially by providing financial or military support.
examples: International aid has been propping up the struggling economy for the past decade.
The central bank intervened to prop up failing financial institutions during the crisis.
Critics argue that foreign investment is merely propping up an authoritarian regime.

entries.definition: To stop by the side of the road in a car or other vehicle.
examples: The police asked the driver to pull over.
Can you pull over here? I need to make a phone call.
We pulled over to check the map.

entries.definition: To manage to stay alive after you have been very ill; to succeed in a very difficult situation.
examples: The doctors weren't sure she would pull through.
Despite the serious injuries, he managed to pull through.
The company pulled through the financial crisis.

entries.definition: To keep telling someone what to do in an unfair or unpleasant way.
examples: I won't let anyone push me around.
The older employees were pushing the new recruits around.
She's tired of being pushed around by her supervisor.

entries.definition: To explain an idea, belief, or argument in a way that is easy to understand.
examples: She put across her proposal with great clarity and confidence.
The documentary puts over the environmental message effectively.
He struggled to put across why the changes were necessary.

entries.definition: To think that something has happened for a particular reason; to attribute something to a cause.
examples: She put his bad mood down to stress at work.
I put the mistake down to inexperience rather than carelessness.
They put the team's success down to excellent preparation.

entries.definition: To fix something such as equipment in the place where it will be used and make it ready to use.
examples: We're having a new kitchen put in next week.
The plumber put in a new boiler yesterday.
They put in double-glazed windows last month.

entries.definition: To spend a particular amount of time doing something or make a particular amount of effort.
examples: She puts in extra hours every week to meet her targets.
You'll need to put in a lot of effort to pass this exam.
The team put in an outstanding performance.

entries.definition: To make something by joining all its parts.
examples: It took me all afternoon to put together the bookshelf.
Can you help me put together this desk?
She put together a beautiful photo album.

entries.definition: To choose people or things to form a team; to produce or organise something.
examples: We need to put together a project team by Friday.
She put together an impressive presentation overnight.
The committee was put together to address the crisis.

entries.definition: To let someone stay in your house.
examples: We can put you up for a few nights if you need.
My parents put me up while I was looking for a flat.
They offered to put up the guests for the wedding.
They put up a new fence around the garden.
The builders are putting up a new block of flats.
We put up some shelves in the living room.

entries.definition: To solve a confusing or complicated problem by thinking carefully about it.
examples: She puzzled out the meaning of the ancient inscription.
It took hours to puzzle out the solution to the riddle.
Engineers finally puzzled out what was causing the malfunction.

entries.definition: To get information on a particular subject by reading a lot about it.
examples: I need to read up on the topic before the meeting.
She read up about the company before her interview.
You should read up on local customs before travelling.

entries.definition: To complete something by doing one final thing.
examples: Let me round off this presentation with a summary of our key recommendations.
The conference was rounded off with a keynote address from the Nobel laureate.
We rounded off the project by publishing our findings in a peer-reviewed journal.

entries.definition: If an organisation or area is run down, its size, importance, and activity is reduced.
examples: The department has been run down over the past decade.
Critics say the government has deliberately run down public services.
The factory was run down before it was finally closed.

entries.definition: To succeed in doing something, but not in a very impressive way; to barely pass.
examples: He only scraped through the exam with the minimum pass mark.
The team scraped through to the finals.
She scraped through her driving test on the third attempt.

entries.definition: To find someone or something by looking for them in a determined way.
examples: She sought out experts to advise on the project.
Young artists seek out mentors to guide their development.
We sought out the best locations for the film.

entries.definition: To state officially how something should be done.
examples: The regulations set down strict guidelines for environmental compliance across all industries.
The constitution sets down the fundamental principles upon which the nation was founded.
New legislation has set down clearer procedures for handling complaints against public officials.

entries.definition: To start doing or working on something in order to achieve an aim.
examples: She set out to prove that the theory was wrong.
They set out to build the most innovative product in the market.
He set out with the intention of changing the industry.

entries.definition: To make a piece of equipment ready for use.
examples: It took me an hour to set up the new printer.
Can you help me set up my email account?
The technician will set up the network tomorrow.
They set up a tent in the garden for the party.
We need to set up the stage before the concert.
He set up the scaffolding around the house.

entries.definition: To make it difficult to hear what someone says by shouting while they are speaking.
examples: The protesters shouted down the politician during his speech.
Don't try to shout down your colleagues during debates.
The controversial speaker was shouted down by angry members of the audience.

entries.definition: To become smaller and thinner than usual and not look fresh and healthy.
examples: The plants shrivelled up in the drought.
Without water, the leaves will shrivel up and die.
The fruit had shrivelled up in the sun.

entries.definition: To choose one person from a group for special attention.
examples: The teacher singled her out for praise.
He felt he was being singled out for criticism.
The award committee singled out three nominees for special recognition.

entries.definition: To think carefully and form an opinion about a person or a situation.
examples: The interviewer quickly sized up each candidate.
He took a moment to size up the situation before responding.
Experienced negotiators know how to size up their opponents.

entries.definition: To leave secretly or without being noticed.
examples: She slipped away from the party without saying goodbye.
The opportunity slipped away before we could act.
He slipped away while everyone was distracted.

entries.definition: To make a careless mistake.
examples: I slipped up and sent the email to the wrong person.
Everyone slips up occasionally—don't be too hard on yourself.
The accountant slipped up with the calculations.

entries.definition: To make yourself look tidy and clean; to improve the appearance of something.
examples: You need to smarten up before the interview.
They smartened up the old building with fresh paint.
He smartened himself up for the wedding.

entries.definition: If you are snowed under, you have too much of something to deal with.
examples: I'm completely snowed under with work at the moment.
The customer service team was snowed under with complaints.
She was snowed under with applications for the position.

entries.definition: To try to find out someone's opinions, ideas, or feelings by talking to them.
examples: I'll sound out the team before making any final decisions.
She sounded out her supervisor about the possibility of a promotion.
He decided to sound out potential investors before launching the startup.

entries.definition: To state your opinion firmly and publicly, especially to protest against or defend something.
examples: She spoke out against the unfair treatment of workers.
More scientists are speaking out about climate change.
He wasn't afraid to speak out in defence of human rights.

entries.definition: If people in a group spread out, they move away from one another so that they cover a large area.
examples: The search party spread out across the forest.
Let's spread out and look for clues separately.
The volunteers spread out to cover more ground.

entries.definition: To appear or be produced suddenly and quickly.
examples: New restaurants are springing up all over the city.
Problems tend to spring up when you least expect them.
Coffee shops have sprung up on every corner.

entries.definition: To be easy to see or notice because of being different.
examples: She always stands out in a crowd with her red hair.
His bright yellow jacket really stands out.
The new design stands out from the competition.

entries.definition: To be much more impressive or important than others; to be conspicuous.
examples: Her talent stood out from an early age.
What makes your application stand out from the rest?
One candidate really stood out during the interviews.

entries.definition: To not allow yourself to be treated badly, especially by someone in authority.
examples: She finally stood up to her bullies.
It takes courage to stand up to injustice.
He stood up to his boss about the unfair working conditions.

entries.definition: To move so that someone can pass you.
examples: She stepped aside to let the elderly man through.
Please step aside and let the passengers exit.
He stepped aside and held the door open.

entries.definition: To leave a job or position so that someone else can take over.
examples: The CEO stepped aside to allow for new leadership.
She stepped aside after twenty years in the role.
It's time for the old guard to step aside for younger talent.

entries.definition: To visit somewhere before continuing to another place.
examples: We stopped off at a café for lunch on the way.
Can we stop off at the supermarket on the way home?
They stopped off in Dubai during their flight to Australia.

entries.definition: To do something that will cause problems in the future; to keep a lot of something.
examples: Avoiding the issue now will only store up problems for later.
She's been storing up resentment for years.
They stored up supplies for the winter.

entries.definition: To manage to produce a quality or a reaction that helps you deal with a difficult situation.
examples: She summoned up the courage to speak in front of the crowd.
He tried to summon up enthusiasm for the project.
I couldn't summon up the energy to go to the gym.

entries.definition: To start or stop a machine, light, etc. working.
examples: Switch on the computer and wait for it to load.
Don't forget to switch off the lights before leaving.
She switched on the TV to watch the news.

entries.definition: To study something very hard, especially for an examination.
examples: I need to swot up on my grammar before the test.
She spent the weekend swotting up on company history.
He's been swotting up on the new regulations.

entries.definition: To look or behave like an older relative.
examples: She takes after her mother in many ways.
My son takes after his grandfather—they're both very patient.
Do you think you take after your mum or your dad?

entries.definition: To separate an object into pieces.
examples: He took apart the engine to find the problem.
The mechanic had to take apart the entire gearbox.
Children love to take apart their toys to see how they work.

entries.definition: To remove one number or quantity from another number or quantity; to subtract.
examples: If you take away three from ten, you get seven.
Take away the discount and the price is still high.
Can you take away these old files from the desk?

entries.definition: To write down information or a statement.
examples: Let me take down your phone number.
The secretary took down the meeting notes.
I need to take down the details before I forget.

entries.definition: To separate a large structure into pieces; to remove from a higher position.
examples: They took down the old scaffolding after construction finished.
The workers took down the exhibition stands overnight.
We need to take down the Christmas decorations.

entries.definition: To accept something as real or true; to understand and remember something.
examples: It took a while to take in the news of his promotion.
There was so much information to take in during the lecture.
She couldn't take in everything they told her.

entries.definition: To trick someone into believing something that is not true.
examples: Sophisticated investors were taken in by the elaborate Ponzi scheme despite numerous warning signs.
The forgery was so convincing that even expert art historians were taken in by it.
Don't be taken in by his charming demeanor—he has ulterior motives.
Despite his scepticism, the experienced investigator was taken in by the elaborate cover story.
Consumers should be vigilant about being taken in by misleading advertising claims.
The fraudster had taken in numerous high-net-worth individuals before being apprehended.

entries.definition: To have a particular amount of time away from work.
examples: I'm taking a week off to visit my family.
She took the afternoon off to go to the dentist.
He rarely takes time off work.

entries.definition: To become successful or popular very fast.
examples: The business really took off after they changed their marketing strategy.
Her career took off when she moved to London.
The app took off and gained millions of users within months.

entries.definition: To accept some work or responsibility.
examples: She took on extra responsibilities at work.
I can't take on any more projects right now.
He took on the role of team leader.

entries.definition: To start to employ someone; to accept some work or responsibility.
examples: The company is taking on fifty new graduates this year.
She took on the role of project manager.
We shouldn't take on more work than we can handle.

entries.definition: To take someone to a place like a cinema or a restaurant and usually pay for them.
examples: He took her out to a fancy restaurant for her birthday.
My parents took us out for ice cream after the game.
I'd like to take you out sometime if you're free.

entries.definition: To begin to do something that someone else was doing; to take control of something.
examples: A larger corporation took over the family business.
She's taking over as department head next month.
Automation is taking over many routine tasks.

entries.definition: To begin to like someone or something; to start doing something as a habit.
examples: The children took to their new teacher immediately.
He's taken to jogging every morning before work.
She really took to the new hobby and spends hours on it.

entries.definition: To talk to someone as if you think they are not as clever or important as you are.
examples: The expert was criticised for talking down to the audience.
Don't talk down to me—I understand the situation perfectly well.
She felt her new boss was constantly talking down to her.

entries.definition: To discuss a problem or a plan.
examples: We should talk over the proposal before making a decision.
Let's talk it over during lunch.
They talked over their differences and made up.

entries.definition: To succeed in persuading someone to agree to something.
examples: It took hours, but I finally talked her round to my point of view.
He managed to talk round the investors despite their initial doubts.
She's stubborn, but I think we can talk her round eventually.

entries.definition: To discuss something in a general way and without dealing with the most important issues.
examples: The committee spent hours talking round the subject without reaching any concrete conclusions.
Politicians are adept at talking round controversial topics to avoid committing to a position.
Stop talking round the issue and tell me directly what you think we should do.

entries.definition: To try using something such as a machine or product to find out whether it works correctly or is satisfactory.
examples: We need to test out the new software before launching it.
I'm testing out a new app for learning languages.
Let's test out the camera before the event.

entries.definition: To consider a problem or decision carefully.
examples: I need time to think over your offer.
Think it over before you make a final decision.
She thought over the situation before responding.

entries.definition: To consider the facts about something in an organised and thorough way.
examples: We need to think through all the possible consequences.
She hadn't thought through the implications of her decision.
Take time to think through your answer before responding.

entries.definition: To invent or imagine something, especially an excuse or a creative solution.
examples: He always thinks up creative excuses for being late.
The marketing team thought up an innovative advertising campaign.
She needs to think up a way to solve this budget problem.

entries.definition: To get rid of something that you no longer want, for example by putting it in a dustbin.
examples: I need to throw out all these old magazines.
Don't throw out those boxes—I might need them!
She threw out her old clothes to make room for new ones.

entries.definition: To force someone to leave a place; to refuse to accept a plan or idea.
examples: The committee threw out the proposal.
They were thrown out of the club for misbehaving.
The judge threw out the case due to lack of evidence.

entries.definition: To help someone to get to the end of a difficult period of time, especially by giving them money until they can get some more.
examples: Could you lend me £50 to tide me over until payday?
The emergency fund will tide us over for a few months.
Her parents helped tide her over during unemployment.

entries.definition: To turn a container upside down so that the things inside it come out.
examples: She tipped up the box and everything fell out.
Tip up the bottle to get the last drops out.
He tipped up the bag to find his keys.

entries.definition: To make a surface look better with small improvements.
examples: The photograph was touched up to remove imperfections.
He touched up the paintwork before putting the house on sale.
She touched up her makeup before the presentation.

entries.definition: To change or develop into something different; to make someone or something change.
examples: The caterpillar turned into a beautiful butterfly.
The conversation turned into a heated argument.
Hard work can turn dreams into reality.

entries.definition: To use all of a supply of something.
examples: We've used up all the milk. Can you buy more?
She used up her holiday days by March.
Don't use up all the hot water!

entries.definition: To leave a meeting before the end, usually because you are angry; to suddenly leave a relationship.
examples: Several delegates walked out in protest during the speech.
She walked out on her husband after discovering his lies.
The union members threatened to walk out of negotiations.

entries.definition: To do something to prevent someone or something from harming you.
examples: She took vitamins to ward off winter colds.
They lit fires to ward off wild animals.
The company took measures to ward off a hostile takeover.

entries.definition: To make food warm again; to prepare for a sport or activity by doing gentle exercises.
examples: I'll warm up some soup for lunch.
Athletes always warm up before a competition.
Can you warm up the milk for the baby?

entries.definition: To gradually become thinner and weaker over a period of time, usually because of an illness.
examples: The illness caused him to waste away over several months.
She watched her father waste away from the disease.
Without proper nutrition, patients can waste away quickly.

entries.definition: To add water to a drink or liquid in order to make it less strong; to dilute.
examples: This juice is too sweet—I need to water it down.
The bartender watered down the drinks.
Don't water down the sauce too much.

entries.definition: To make something such as a statement or newspaper article less offensive, powerful, or detailed.
examples: The original proposal was significantly watered down after pressure from industry lobbyists.
Critics accused the committee of watering down the recommendations to appease special interests.
The final report was so watered down that it failed to address the most pressing concerns.

entries.definition: To make someone gradually lose their energy or confidence.
examples: The constant criticism wore her down emotionally.
Years of stress had worn him down completely.
The long hours were wearing down even the most resilient workers.

entries.definition: To use something a lot so that it no longer works or can no longer be used.
examples: I've worn out three pairs of trainers this year.
The carpet is starting to wear out in the hallway.
These jeans are completely worn out.

entries.definition: To spend time in a relaxed way when you have nothing else to do.
examples: We whiled away the afternoon playing chess.
She whiled away the hours reading novels on the beach.
They whiled away the evening with pleasant conversation.

entries.definition: To relax after a period of excitement or worry.
examples: I like to wind down with a good book before bed.
After the stressful week, we needed to wind down.
She winds down by listening to soft music.

entries.definition: To end or finish something gradually; to relax after a period of activity.
examples: The company is winding down its European operations.
As the project winds down, we'll reassign staff.
It takes me an hour to wind down after a busy day.

entries.definition: To damage a vehicle so badly that it is not worth repairing; to accept a loss as irrecoverable.
examples: The insurance company wrote off the car after the accident.
They had to write off a significant amount of bad debt.
Don't write off the investment just yet—it may recover.

entries.definition: To decide that someone or something will not succeed and stop giving them your attention and energy.
examples: Many analysts were quick to write off the new political party, but it gained significant momentum.
It would be premature to write off the reform movement despite its recent setbacks.
The international community had effectively written off the peace process until the breakthrough negotiations.

entries.definition: To find the total of several numbers or amounts.
examples: Can you add up the prices for me?
I added up the costs before buying anything.
She added up the points in her notebook.

entries.definition: To explode.
examples: Luckily, the old gas canister didn't blow up.
The fireworks blew up in the sky.
The bomb disposal team made sure nothing would blow up.

entries.definition: If a machine or vehicle breaks down, it stops working.
examples: Our car broke down on the motorway.
My laptop broke down during the presentation.
The lift broke down again this morning.

entries.definition: To enter a building illegally, usually by force.
examples: Someone tried to break in last night.
Thieves broke into the office at dawn.
They called the police after someone broke in.

entries.definition: To take care of a child until they become an adult.
examples: She brought up three children on her own.
They were brought up in a small town.
His grandparents helped bring him up.

entries.definition: To increase or develop something gradually.
examples: You need to build up your confidence.
He built up his business over many years.
These exercises build up leg strength.

entries.definition: To phone someone again later.
examples: I'll call you back after my meeting.
She called me back within five minutes.
Can you call back tomorrow morning?

entries.definition: To cancel an event or plan.
examples: They called off the concert because of the weather.
We had to call off the match.
The meeting was called off at the last minute.

entries.definition: To become calmer or to make someone calmer.
examples: Please calm down and tell me what happened.
She finally calmed down after the argument.
Music helps me calm down.

entries.definition: To continue doing something.
examples: Please carry on with your work.
She carried on talking despite the noise.
We'll carry on after lunch.

entries.definition: To reach the same point, level, or standard as someone else.
examples: I need to catch up with my classmates.
He ran faster to catch up with us.
She's trying to catch up with all her reading.

entries.definition: To become happier, or to make someone happier.
examples: Cheer up! Things will get better.
The sunshine cheered everyone up.
I sent her flowers to cheer her up.

entries.definition: To tidy a place and make it clean and neat.
examples: Please clear up the kitchen after dinner.
We cleared up the living room before guests arrived.
I'll clear up while you rest.

entries.definition: To find something by chance.
examples: I came across an old photo yesterday.
She came across a useful article online.
We came across a quiet café in town.

entries.definition: To return from a place.
examples: When did you come back from Italy?
He came back from work late.
Call me when you come back from holiday.

entries.definition: Used to tell someone to hurry up.
examples: Come on, we're going to be late!
Come on, the bus is leaving.
Come on! Move faster.

entries.definition: To be published or become available for people to buy or see.
examples: Her new book comes out next month.
That film came out in 2019.
When does the next issue come out?

entries.definition: To draw a line through written words to show they are wrong or not needed.
examples: Cross out the wrong answer.
He crossed out the sentence and rewrote it.
Please cross out your old address.

entries.definition: To reduce the amount of something you do, eat, or use.
examples: I'm trying to cut down on sugar.
He cut down on coffee last month.
We should cut down on plastic use.

entries.definition: To disconnect something (electricity/phone), or to remove something by cutting.
examples: They cut off our electricity by mistake.
The line was cut off during the call.
She cut off the damaged part of the plant.

entries.definition: To fasten buttons or a zip on clothes.
examples: Do up your coat—it's cold outside.
He did up his jacket before leaving.
Can you do up this zip for me?

entries.definition: To eat in a restaurant instead of at home.
examples: We usually eat out on Fridays.
Let's eat out tonight.
They ate out after the concert.

entries.definition: To trip and fall to the ground.
examples: Be careful—you might fall down.
He fell down and hurt his knee.
I nearly fell down on the wet floor.

entries.definition: To have an argument with someone and stop being friendly.
examples: I fell out with my best friend last year.
They fell out over a small misunderstanding.
Have you ever fallen out with a colleague?

entries.definition: To add information in the spaces on a form or document.
examples: Please fill in this form.
I filled in all the details online.
He forgot to fill in his phone number.

entries.definition: To make something completely full.
examples: Fill up the bottle with water.
The room filled up quickly.
Can you fill up the car before we leave?

entries.definition: To discover information.
examples: I need to find out what happened.
She found out the truth yesterday.
We'll find out the results tomorrow.

entries.definition: start a journey
examples: As the sun rose, we took down our tents and set out across the desert.

entries.definition: start doing sth in a determined or enthusiastic way
examples: We only had a few hours to get the party ready so we set to work quickly.

entries.definition: start (a business, organisation, etc)
examples: You’re such a good cook that I think you should set up a restaurant.

entries.definition: become calm after being upset, etc
examples: When his mum left him at school on the first day, Charlie was quite upset, but he soon settled down and started to enjoy himself.

entries.definition: stay in one place or get married and live quietly
examples: She spent her twenties travelling round the world and then settled down in a quiet village in Sussex.

entries.definition: take sb on a tour of a place
examples: Let me show you round the garden.

entries.definition: try to attract people’s attention and make them admire you (usually used negatively)
examples: My sister thinks she’s a good singer and she’s always showing off when people come to visit.

entries.definition: decrease speed
examples: The train started to slow down as it approached the station.

entries.definition: increase speed
examples: I realised that I might not have enough time to finish the exam so I started to speed up.

entries.definition: represent (for abbreviations and symbols)
examples: BBC stands for ‘British Broadcasting Corporation’.

entries.definition: put up with
examples: I wouldn’t stand for it if someone spoke that rudely to me.

entries.definition: do sb’s job for them while they are not available
examples: Mr Howard will be standing in for Mrs O’Connell while she’s ill.

entries.definition: be easy to see because of being different
examples: Molly has bright red hair, so she always stands out in a crowd!

entries.definition: support in an argument or fight
examples: I know that my best friend will always stand up for me.

entries.definition: surprise (usually in passive voice)
examples: We were all taken aback by the news.

entries.definition: look or behave like an older relative
examples: George really takes after his grandfather, doesn’t he?

entries.definition: write down what someone says
examples: Could I just take down your details?

entries.definition: trick sb into believing sth that is not true
examples: The man at the door claimed to be a policeman, but the old woman wasn’t taken in.

entries.definition: become successful or popular very fast
examples: The iPod has really taken off over the last couple of years.

entries.definition: leave the ground
examples: As the plane took off, I looked down at the houses below us.

entries.definition: remove (a piece of clothing)
examples: Carl took off his coat and started to warm himself by the fire.

entries.definition: start to employ
examples: There’s so much work to do in the office that maybe we should take on a secretary.

entries.definition: accept (work or responsibility)
examples: I haven’t got time to take on any more projects at the moment.

entries.definition: take control of (a business, etc)
examples: When Mr Barker retired, his daughter took over the family business.

entries.definition: begin to like
examples: The class took to Mrs Openshaw straight away.

entries.definition: start (as a habit)
examples: I’ve taken to going for a five-mile run every morning.

entries.definition: start (a hobby, sport, etc)
examples: Why don’t you take up a hobby, like collecting something?

entries.definition: fill an amount of space/time
examples: Studying takes up most of my time at the moment.

entries.definition: destroy or remove (for buildings, statues, etc)
examples: That beautiful old building was torn down to make way for a new supermarket.

entries.definition: rip into pieces
examples: Robin suddenly grabbed the letter out of my hand and tore it up.

entries.definition: consider
examples: When I asked Theresa to marry me, she said she’d have to think it over.

entries.definition: get rid of, discard
examples: The CD wouldn’t play any more, so I threw it away.

entries.definition: put on (a piece of clothing) to see how it looks and whether it fits
examples: Debra took the skirt into the changing room to try it on.

entries.definition: experiment with
examples: England need to try out a few new players before the World Cup starts.

entries.definition: not accept (an offer, request, etc)
examples: I applied to join the Air Force, but they turned me down because of my eyesight.

entries.definition: change into sth different
examples: It started as the holiday of a lifetime but soon turned into a nightmare.

entries.definition: stop a machine working
examples: Would you please turn that stereo off and get your homework done?

entries.definition: develop in a particular way or have a particular result
examples: I was late for the interview, but it turned out all right in the end and I got the job.

entries.definition: turn a page so that the other side is towards you
examples: If you all turn over, you’ll see a table that shows how sales have gone this year.

entries.definition: go back in the opposite direction
examples: I realised I’d forgotten my phone so I turned round and went back to get it.

entries.definition: appear unexpectedly or without making a firm arrangement
examples: Victor hadn’t been invited to the party but he just turned up anyway.

entries.definition: stop being effective (for a drug, etc)
examples: My arm started to hurt again as the effects of the painkiller wore off.

entries.definition: become old and unusable
examples: I had to get a new pair of trainers because my old pair had worn out.

entries.definition: find the solution to a problem, etc
examples: If you look at the context, maybe you can work out what the word means.
